title: Bio-Inspired Event-Based Visual Servoing for Ground Robots
abstract: 生物感知系统天生具有适应性，能够过滤恒定刺激并优先处理相对变化，这很可能提升了计算与代谢效率。受多种动物主动感知行为的启发，本文提出了一种新型的基于事件的地面机器人视觉伺服框架。利用动态视觉传感器，我们证明通过对结构化对数强度变化模式产生的异步事件流施加固定空间核函数，所得净事件通量可在解析层面分离出特定运动状态。我们为此事件率估计器建立了广义理论边界，并证明线性和二次空间分布可分别分离出机器人的速度及位置-速度乘积。基于这些特性，我们采用多模式刺激直接合成非线性状态反馈项，完全无需传统状态估计。为克服事件传感在平衡点不可避免的线性可观测性损失，我们提出了一种仿生主动感知极限环控制器。在1/10比例自主地面车辆上的实验验证证实了所提直接感知方法的有效性、极低延迟性和计算高效性。

,

∗

†

Abstract
Biological sensory systems are inherently adaptive, filtering out constant stimuli and prioritizing relative changes, likely enhancing computational and metabolic efficiency. Inspired by active sensing behaviors across a wide range of animals, this paper presents a novel event-based visual servoing framework for ground robots. Utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS), we demonstrate that by applying a fixed spatial kernel to the asynchronous event stream generated from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns, the resulting net event flux analytically isolates specific kinematic states. We establish a generalized theoretical bound for this event rate estimator and show that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate the robot’s velocity and position-velocity product, respectively. Leveraging these properties, we employ a multi-pattern stimulus to directly synthesize a nonlinear state-feedback term entirely without traditional state estimation. To overcome the inescapable loss of linear observability at equilibrium inherent in event sensing, we propose a bio-inspired active sensing limit-cycle controller. Experimental validation on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle confirms the efficacy, extreme low-latency, and computational efficiency of the proposed direct-sensing approach.
I
Introduction
Biological sensors function primarily as relative detectors that attenuate constant (i.e., low-frequency or “DC”) stimuli while emphasizing changes (“AC”)
[
1
]
, a characteristic known as sensory adaptation.
To prevent stationary objects from becoming “invisible” due to these high-pass sensors, animals execute continuous bursts of back-and-forth swimming motions
[
2
,
3
,
4
]
that convert static spatial gradients into detectable temporal signals
[
5
]
. Examples include electric fish
[
2
,
3
,
6
]
, rodents actively whisking to induce friction-based micro-accelerations
[
7
]
, insects relying on flight-driven optic flow
[
1
]
, blind cave fish gliding to distort pressure waves
[
8
]
, and the human visual system employing involuntary microsaccades to prevent perceptual fading
[
9
]
.
Such adaptive, high-pass sensing, while highly efficient and low-latency, poses significant design challenges for robotics. For example, in mobile robotics, conventional state estimation and control methods fail under these conditions, as proved in
[
10
]
. Traditional sensors, such as frame-based cameras, IMUs, and LiDAR, rely on power-hungry, absolute sensing. They capture data at fixed intervals regardless of whether a scene is dynamic or static. For this reason, replicating bio-inspired principles is crucial for future developments, which requires a fundamental shift from these traditional methods to relative, event-driven sensing
[
11
,
12
,
13
]
.
In this work, we employ a neuromorphic event camera, also known as a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS). Serving as a technological analog to biological vision, the DVS samples light based on scene dynamics rather than an arbitrary external clock
[
13
]
. Instead of capturing full images at fixed frame rates, each pixel operates independently and asynchronously. A pixel triggers a spike (an address-event) only when its local logarithmic brightness changes by a preset threshold, producing a continuous stream of event tuples containing the pixel coordinates, a microsecond-resolution timestamp, and the polarity of the change (brightness increase or decrease)
[
14
,
15
]
. Consequently, the DVS offers several key advantages over standard cameras, including microsecond temporal resolution, high dynamic range (HDR), and immunity to motion blur
[
13
,
16
]
. Furthermore, by transmitting data exclusively during motion, the DVS performs inherent hardware-level compression, effectively filtering out redundant static backgrounds.
Motivated by these biological principles, we aim to develop robust Event-Based Visual Servoing (EBVS) for embodied intelligent agents, such as ground robots, drones, and collaborative factory robots. EBVS entails moving either an event camera or the camera’s visual target such that the generated event stream drives the camera to a desired region
[
17
]
.
Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) is widely established in the literature, traditionally relying on frame-based cameras to extract and match geometric features against a stored “desired” view
[
18
,
19
,
14
]
.
Kernel-Based Visual Servoing (KBVS)
[
20
,
21
]
bypasses explicit feature extraction and segmentation by applying a spatial kernel that processes the entire image as a continuous signal. This kernel-based paradigm serves as the foundational framework for our proposed approach to EBVS.
To reduce computational overhead, Garcia et al.
[
22
]
emulated an event-based camera to trigger IBVS updates only when tracked features crossed spatial boundaries. Similarly, Gil et al.
[
23
]
utilized event cameras for high-speed feature tracking, falling back to frame-based vision when necessary. Pushing toward fully event-driven perception, Muthusamy et al.
[
17
]
replaced conventional vision processing with event-based feature tracking while retaining traditional IBVS control laws. More recently, learning-based approaches have emerged; for example, Vinod et al.
[
24
]
employed deep neural networks to map fused RGB images and simulated event streams directly to motor commands.
In this work, we propose a purely event-driven and computationally efficient EBVS framework that applies a spatial kernel to predefined intensity patterns. The objective is to regulate the camera position to a desired region of the displayed pattern. Our approach avoids explicit feature extraction, feature tracking, and state estimation. In fact, it relies solely on the net number of events generated by the event camera, leading to a highly computationally efficient implementation. To maintain system observability and overcome sensory adaptation, we adapt the control-theoretic framework proposed in
[
10
]
. This allows us to formulate a direct sensor-to-motor active sensing control law with proven stability conditions.
Our overall contribution is a novel EBVS approach that overcomes the lack of local observability inherent in event-based sensing using a bio-inspired, limit-cycle-based control law with guaranteed (local) exponential convergence
1
1
1
The GitHub repository provides access to video demonstrations, example codes, and datasets associated with this work:
https://github.com/SiamiLab/BioInspiredEBVS
.
.

V
Conclusion and Future Work
This paper presented a purely event-driven, bio-inspired visual servoing framework that bypasses computationally expensive feature extraction by directly coupling spatial intensity patterns with the net event rate of a dynamic vision sensor. By demonstrating that specific spatial profiles isolate distinct kinematic states, we successfully synthesized direct nonlinear state feedback and maintained system observability at equilibrium using a limit-cycle active sensing controller. Future work will focus on extending this framework to multi-dimensional visual servoing and exploring its robustness against dynamic targets in unstructured environments. A limitation of the current approach is that it relies on a specially designed visual scene and simple rectangular kernels, but future work will investigate how to relax the former assumption by using richer kernel geometries as in prior work for traditional cameras
[
20
]
. Furthermore, we plan to investigate alternative active sensing control architectures to optimize the trade-off between sensory effort and tracking accuracy. Preliminary investigations indicate that replacing the

preview_text:
Biological sensory systems are inherently adaptive, filtering out constant stimuli and prioritizing relative changes, likely enhancing computational and metabolic efficiency. Inspired by active sensing behaviors across a wide range of animals, this paper presents a novel event-based visual servoing framework for ground robots. Utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS), we demonstrate that by applying a fixed spatial kernel to the asynchronous event stream generated from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns, the resulting net event flux analytically isolates specific kinematic states. We establish a generalized theoretical bound for this event rate estimator and show that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate the robot's velocity and position-velocity product, respectively. Leveraging these properties, we employ a multi-pattern stimulus to directly synthesize a nonlinear state-feedback term entirely without traditional state estimation. To overcome the inescapable loss of linear observability at equilibrium inherent in event sensing, we propose a bio-inspired active sensing limit-cycle controller. Experimental validation on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle confirms the efficacy, extreme low-latency, and computational efficiency of the proposed direct-sensing approach.

Bio-Inspired Event-Based Visual Servoing for Ground Robots
